title: Go Modules and Imports
content:
### Importing Packages

Use `import` to access exported names from other packages.

### Import Syntax

```go
// Single import
import "fmt"

// Multiple imports
import (
    "fmt"
    "math"
    "strings"
)

// Local package
import "example.com/myproject/calculator"

// Third-party package
import "github.com/gorilla/mux"
```

### Import Aliases

```go
import (
    "crypto/rand"
    mrand "math/rand" // alias to avoid conflict
)

func main() {
    mrand.Intn(100)
}
```

### Dot Import

Import names directly into current namespace (use sparingly):

```go
import . "fmt"

func main() {
    Println("Hello") // no fmt. prefix
}
```

### Blank Import

For side effects (init functions):

```go
import _ "image/png" // registers PNG decoder
import _ "net/http/pprof" // enables profiling
```

### Import Path Conventions

| Path Pattern | Description |
|--------------|-------------|
| `std` | Standard library |
| `example.com/pkg` | Your own package |
| `github.com/user/repo` | GitHub hosted |
| `golang.org/x/tools` | Go sub-repositories |

### Go Workspace (pre-1.16)

Before modules, Go used `GOPATH`:

```
$GOPATH/
  src/
    github.com/user/repo/
  bin/
  pkg/
```

### Module Path Best Practices

```
// Repository-based: good for published modules
github.com/username/project

// Domain-based: good for private code
example.com/team/project

// Simple path: for local experiments
myproject
```

### Import Resolution Order

1. Standard library (no path prefix)
2. Module dependencies (from go.mod)
3. Local packages (relative to module)

> **Key Point:** Import paths must be unique. Use aliases when names conflict. Blank imports are for side effects only. Standard library packages have no path prefix.

quiz.question: How do you resolve naming conflicts between imported packages?
What does a blank import (_) do?
